name: Network Port Scanning Detection
description: Detects potential port scanning activity when a single source IP attempts to connect to multiple destination ports within a short time period, indicating reconnaissance behavior.
author: Security Team

params.subject: WARNING: Port Scanning Activity Detected

note:
## Investigation Guide

### Initial Analysis
1. Identify the source IP address and check if it's internal or external
2. Determine if the IP belongs to authorized scanning tools
3. Review the specific ports being targeted:
   - Well-known ports (80, 443, 22, 3389) suggest standard reconnaissance
   - Uncommon high ports might indicate advanced attacker knowledge
   - Sequential port scanning indicates automated tools (nmap, masscan)

### Port Categories to Analyze
- **Administrative:** 22 (SSH), 3389 (RDP), 23 (Telnet)
- **Web Services:** 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 8080, 8443
- **Database:** 3306 (MySQL), 5432 (PostgreSQL), 1433 (MSSQL)
- **File Transfer:** 21 (FTP), 445 (SMB)
- **Email:** 25 (SMTP), 110 (POP3), 143 (IMAP)

### Response Actions
- [ ] Verify if source is authorized security scanner
- [ ] Check threat intelligence for IP reputation
- [ ] Review if any ports responded positively
- [ ] Analyze time pattern (distributed vs. rapid)
- [ ] Block IP temporarily if external and suspicious
- [ ] Monitor for exploitation attempts on discovered services
- [ ] Review vulnerability status of scanned services
- [ ] Check for any successful connections after the scan

### Escalation Criteria
- Source IP has known malicious history
- Scan followed by exploitation attempts
- Internal IP performing unauthorized scanning
- Scanning sensitive/production systems
- Pattern matches known attack frameworks (Metasploit, etc.)
